file_name: Abbas and Celebi - 2019 - DermoDeep-A classification of melanoma-nevus skin lesions using multi-feature fusion of visual featu.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed 'DermoDeep', a computer-aided diagnosis system that uses a five-layer deep neural network architecture and multi-feature fusion of visual features to classify melanoma versus nevus skin lesions from dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents the development and validation of a new classification system called 'DermoDeep' using a five-layer deep neural network architecture. It reports original experimental results, including training on 2800 dermoscopy images, and provides specific performance metrics (AUC: 0.96, SE: 93%, SP: 95%) compared to state-of-the-art methods, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: DermoDeep-A classification of melanoma-nevus skin lesions using multi-feature fusion of visual features and deep neural network
brief_summary: This study developed DermoDeep, a computer-aided diagnosis system for classifying melanoma versus nevus pigmented skin lesions using dermoscopy images. The system employs a five-layer deep neural network architecture combined with multi-feature fusion of visual features extracted in a perceptual-oriented color space. The model was trained and evaluated on a dataset of 2800 region-of-interest images sourced from Skin-EDRA, ISIC, DermNet, and Ph2-datasets. Performance was assessed using sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and area under the receiver operating characteristic (AUC) curve via 10-fold cross-validation. The DermoDeep system achieved an AUC of 0.96, sensitivity of 93%, specificity of 95%, and accuracy of 95%. These results were statistically significantly higher than those of state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating the system's potential to assist dermatologists in screening processes.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary classes in the binary classification task. The study used 1400 malignant lesions in total (250 from Skin-EDRA, 800 from ISIC, 300 from DermNet, and 30 from Ph2-dataset).

One of the two primary classes in the binary classification task. The study used 1400 benign/nevus lesions in total (250 from Skin-EDRA, 800 from ISIC, 300 from DermNet, and 70 from Ph2-dataset). Note: The Ph2-dataset subset included both common and atypical nevi, but they were grouped into the 'benign' class for the experiment.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of pigmented skin lesions into melanoma (malignant) or nevus (benign) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper develops 'DermoDeep', a computer-aided diagnosis system for binary classification of melanoma versus nevus skin lesions using dermoscopy images. The model uses a five-layer deep neural network with a softmax linear classifier to discriminate between the two classes. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity (93%), specificity (95%), accuracy (95%), and AUC (0.96) on a dataset of 2800 images (1400 melanoma, 1400 nevus).

datasets_used.name: Skin-EDRA
datasets_used.description: A CD resource collected from two European university hospitals as part of the EDRA-CDROM. Contains dermoscopy images with a resolution of 768 × 512 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the DermoDeep system (part of the 50% training split).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate validation set; 10-fold cross-validation was used on the combined dataset.
use.testing: Used for testing the DermoDeep system (part of the 50% testing split).

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: Collected from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration. Contains dermoscopy images with a resolution of 768 × 512 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the DermoDeep system (part of the 50% training split).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate validation set; 10-fold cross-validation was used on the combined dataset.
use.testing: Used for testing the DermoDeep system (part of the 50% testing split).

datasets_used.name: DermNet
datasets_used.description: Acquired from the Department of Dermatology, University of Auckland. Contains dermoscopy images with a resolution of 768 × 512 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the DermoDeep system (part of the 50% training split).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate validation set; 10-fold cross-validation was used on the combined dataset.

datasets_used.name: Ph2-dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image database for research and benchmarking. Contains dermoscopy images with a resolution of 768 × 560 pixels. The study selected a subset of 100 images from the original 200 (which included 80 common nevi, 80 atypical nevi, and 40 melanomas).

use.training: Used for training the DermoDeep system (part of the 50% training split).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate validation set; 10-fold cross-validation was used on the combined dataset.
use.testing: Used for testing the DermoDeep system (part of the 50% testing split).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to evaluate the performance of the DermoDeep system and compare it with state-of-the-art methods (Premaladha_deep and Jeremy_deep).
To evaluate the statistical significance of the difference between the AUC of DermoDeep and other techniques.
To quantify the overall discriminative capability of classifiers and compare performance via sensitivity and specificity.
Used as a performance index to measure classification accuracy and compare DermoDeep against other systems.
Statistical measures calculated to evaluate the classifier's performance in terms of true positive rate and false positive rate.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference between the AUC of the DermoDeep system and other techniques.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference between the AUC of the DermoDeep system and other techniques.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The difference between the AUC of DermoDeep (0.96) and other techniques was found to be statistically significant with a p-value less than 0.001.

Combined Dataset (2800 images: Skin-EDRA.notes: The difference between the AUC of DermoDeep and other techniques was statistically significant (p < 0.001). DermoDeep outperformed Premaladha_deep and Jeremy_deep.

architecture_used.main_model: DermoDeep (a five-layer deep neural network architecture combining multi-feature fusion with Recurrent Neural Networks)

components.description: Extracts hand-crafted visual features in a perceptual-oriented CIEL*a*b* color space. Color coherence features derived using bag-of-words (BoW) with k-means clustering on SURF features. Texture features extracted using Stack-based-AutoEncoders (SAE).

Extracts deep features using a CNN model with structure: conv5 32 → conv5 64 → conv3 128 → Pooling 2. Convolutional layers use kernel size (k × k) and stride length 2. Stack-based-Autoencoders (SAE) used as encoder (fully connected MLP 600x600) and decoder with ReLU activation for 450 SGD epochs.
Combines features from VD-L and DF-L using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to reduce dimensionality. 750-element eigenvalues selected.
Uses Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) to optimize features from FF-L. RNN processes input vector sequence through N recurrently connected hidden layers.
Softmax linear classifier for binary classification.

feature_extraction_methods: Bag-of-Words (BoW) with k-means clustering on SURF features

training_details.epochs: 450 SGD epochs for SAE; 300 iterations for RNN deep learning neural network classifier

classification_or_output_head.output_description: Outputs predictive probability distribution P(ft + 1| Ot) parameterized by softmax function

preprocessing_techniques: Color space conversion: RGB images converted to perceptual-oriented CIEL*a*b* color space using CAT2000 advanced distance metric.
Resizing: Images automatically resized to 600 × 600 pixels.
Cropping/ROI Extraction: Circular region-of-interest (ROI) images of size 400 × 400 pixels automatically extracted from the center of the image.
conclusions: The proposed DermoDeep system, which fuses multi-feature visual descriptors with a five-layer deep neural network architecture, achieved high classification performance for differentiating melanoma from nevus skin lesions.
DermoDeep achieved an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.96, sensitivity of 93%, specificity of 95%, and accuracy of 95% on a dataset of 2800 dermoscopy images.
The performance of DermoDeep was statistically significantly higher than state-of-the-art methods (Premaladha_deep and Jeremy_deep), with a p-value less than 0.001.
The use of a perceptual-oriented CIEL*a*b* color space and the fusion of visual features contributed to the improved classification accuracy compared to systems relying on non-uniform color spaces.
The DermoDeep system has the potential to assist dermatologists, particularly less experienced practitioners, by providing a second opinion during the screening process for pigmented skin lesions.
The system can serve as a valuable training tool for medical students and inexpert practitioners due to its ability to recognize large collections of pigmented skin lesions using visual attributes.

A limitation of the DermoDeep system is its computational time (2.1 seconds per image), which is higher than compared CNN-based systems, attributed to the introduction of hand-crafted visual features in a perceptual-oriented color space.
important_remarks: The study relies on a relatively small dataset of 2800 images (1400 melanoma, 1400 nevus) aggregated from four heterogeneous sources (Skin-EDRA, ISIC, DermNet, Ph2), which may limit generalizability.

The system exhibits high computational latency (2.1 seconds per image), which is significantly slower than compared CNN-based systems (Premaladha_deep and Jeremy_deep) due to the use of hand-crafted visual features in a perceptual-oriented color space.
The paper acknowledges that classification accuracy is negatively affected by artifacts such as hair and dermoscopy gel pixels, which were present in the dataset but not explicitly removed.
The study does not report external validation on a completely independent hold-out dataset; performance was evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation on the combined dataset.
The Ph2-dataset subset used in the study grouped 'common nevi' and 'atypical nevi' together into a single 'benign' class, potentially masking performance differences between benign subtypes.
The authors note that the current MATLAB implementation is computationally expensive and suggest future work in C++ with GPU parallelism to improve speed.

The study claims statistical significance (p < 0.001) for the difference in AUC between DermoDeep and state-of-the-art methods, but does not provide detailed confidence intervals for the performance metrics.
